questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q:
Paragraph A thin uniform rod of length $\ell$ and mass $m$ lies on a frictionless fixed horizontal surface. It is struck with a quick horizontal blow (directed perpendicular to the rod) at a distance $x$ from the center of rod. A dot is painted on the horizontal surface at a distance $d$ from the initial position of the center of the rod.
Question Let $v$ be the speed of centre of mass and $\omega$ be the angular speed of the rod just after the blow. Then the value of $\frac{\omega}{\mathrm{v}}$ is :
questions.solution:
$\Delta \mathrm{L}=\int \tau \mathrm{dt}=\int \mathrm{x} \mathrm{Fdt}=\mathrm{x} \Delta \mathrm{P}$ (because x is essentially constant during the quick blow) since, the rod starts at rest, the final values therefore satisfy $L=x P$.
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{12} \mathrm{~m} \ell^2 \omega=\mathrm{xmv} \Rightarrow \frac{\omega}{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{12 \mathrm{x}}{\ell^2}$     ....(1)
Another expression for $\frac{\omega}{v}$ is obtained from the given information that rod makes one revolution by the time centre reaches the dot.
${\omega}t =2 \pi \text { and } \mathrm{vt}=\mathrm{d} \quad \Rightarrow \frac{\omega}{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{2 \pi}{\mathrm{~d}}$       ......(2)
from equation 1 and 2: $\frac{12 \mathrm{x}}{\ell^2}=\frac{2 \pi}{d}$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{x}=\frac{\pi{\ell^2}}{6 \mathrm{~d}}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Paragraph: A simplified model of a bicycle of mass $M$ has two tyres that each comes into contact with the ground at a point. The wheelbase of this bicycle (the distance between the points of contact with the ground) is $w$, and the center of mass of the bicycle is located midway between the tyres and at height $h$ above the ground. Air resistance may be ignored.Case-2 : The bicycle is moving to the right, but slowing down at a constant rate. The acceleration has a magnitude a. Assume that the coefficient of sliding friction between each tyre and the ground is $\mu$, and that both tyres are skidding (sliding without rotating). Express your answers in terms of $\mathrm{w}, \mathrm{h}$, M and g .Question: What is the maximum value of $\mu$ so that both tyres remain in contact with the ground?
questions.solution: $\mathrm{f}_1+\mathrm{f}_2=\mathrm{f}=\mathrm{ma}$ $\ldots(i)$ $\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{N}_1+\mathrm{N}_2=\mathrm{mg}$ $\ldots(ii)$ $\left(\vec{\tau}_{\mathrm{net}}\right)_{\mathrm{cm}}=0$ $\left(\mathrm{f}_1+\mathrm{f}_2\right) \mathrm{h}+\mathrm{N}_1 \mathrm{w}-\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{w}=0$ $\ldots(iii)$ $\mathrm{f}_1+\mathrm{f}_2=\mathrm{f}=\mu \mathrm{N}_1+\mu \mathrm{N}_2$ $\ldots(iv)$ From (ii) \& (iii) $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{N}_2=\frac{1}{2}\left[\mathrm{mg}-\frac{2 \mathrm{f} \mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{w}}\right] \\ & \mathrm{N}_1=\frac{1}{2}\left[\mathrm{mg}+\frac{2 \mathrm{f} \mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{w}}\right]\end{aligned}$ For both the tyres to remain in contact with the ground, $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{N}_2 \geq 0 \\ & \mathrm{mg} \geq \frac{2 \mathrm{~h}}{\mathrm{w}}(\mathrm{mg}) \mu \quad[\text { using (iv) }]\end{aligned}$ $\mu \leq \frac{w}{2 h}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.q: Paragraph: A simplified model of a bicycle of mass $M$ has two tyres that each comes into contact with the ground at a point. The wheelbase of this bicycle (the distance between the points of contact with the ground) is $w$, and the center of mass of the bicycle is located midway between the tyres and at height $h$ above the ground. Air resistance may be ignored. Case-2 : The bicycle is moving to the right, but slowing down at a constant rate. The acceleration has a magnitude a. Assume that the coefficient of sliding friction between each tyre and the ground is $\mu$, and that both tyres are skidding (sliding without rotating). Express your answers in terms of $\mathrm{w}, \mathrm{h}$, M and g .Question:What is the maximum value of a so that both tyres remain in contact with the ground?

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q:
Paragraph: A simplified model of a bicycle of mass $M$ has two tyres that each comes into contact with the ground at a point. The wheelbase of this bicycle (the distance between the points of contact with the ground) is $w$, and the center of mass of the bicycle is located midway between the tyres and at height $h$ above the ground. Air resistance may be ignored.
Case-1 : Assume that cycle is moving rightwards \& has acceleration a. Both tyres roll purely.Question: The direction of friction on the tyres is
options: forward on front \& backward on rear
forward on rear \& backward on front.
questions.solution:
Friction Force on the Wheels during Pedaling Friction Force on the Front Wheel The front wheel is connected to the rest of the bicycle by a rod passing through its centre (axle). The torque on the wheel about its centre by the force coming from the rest of the bicycle is zero. Thus, pedaling can give linear velocity to the front wheel but cannot rotate it.
Let us analyse a simple situation to get the direction of friction force on the front wheel. What will happen if a wheel translating with speed $u$ is gently placed on a rough surface ? Every point on the wheel, including centre C and contact point P translates with the same speed u . Thus, P moves towards right relative to the surface. To oppose this, frictional force at P acts towards the left. The frictional force retards the velocity of the centre. The torque about C due to frictional force gives clockwise angular acceleration and the wheel starts rotating in clockwise direction. If the coefficient of friction is sufficiently large then the wheel will start rolling without slipping after sometime. At this instant, velocity of P relative to the surface becomes zero. The frictional force will become zero and the wheel continues to roll without slipping, $v=\omega r$.Friction Force on the Rear Wheel The rear wheel is connected to rest of the bicycle by a rod passing through its centre and a chain connected to the pedals (see figure). Pressing the pedal increases tension in the upper portion of the chain. This tension gives rise to clockwise torque and wheel starts rotating in clockwise direction.
Let us analyse a simple situation. What will happen if a wheel rotating with angular velocity $\omega_0$ is gently placed on a rough surface ? Initial velocity of the contact point P is leftward relative to the surface. To oppose this, frictional force at P acts towards the right. The frictional force increases the velocity of C . The torque about C due to frictional force is anti-clockwise. This torque gives anti-clockwise angular acceleration. If the coefficient of friction is sufficiently large then the wheel will start rolling without slipping after some time. At this instant, velocity of P relative to the surface becomes zero. After this, the wheel continues to roll without slipping, $v=\omega r$.
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q:
Paragraph A thin uniform rod of length $\ell$ and mass $m$ lies on a frictionless fixed horizontal surface. It is struck with a quick horizontal blow (directed perpendicular to the rod) at a distance $x$ from the center of rod. A dot is painted on the horizontal surface at a distance $d$ from the initial position of the center of the rod.
Question For the rod to make one complete revolution by the time the center reaches the dot, the value of x (in terms of $\ell$ and d) should be:
questions.solution:
$\Delta \mathrm{L}=\int \tau \mathrm{dt}=\int \mathrm{x} \mathrm{Fdt}=\mathrm{x} \Delta \mathrm{P}$ (because x is essentially constant during the quick blow) since, the rod starts at rest, the final values therefore satisfy $L=x P$.
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{12} \mathrm{~m} \ell^2 \omega=\mathrm{xmv} \Rightarrow \frac{\omega}{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{12 \mathrm{x}}{\ell^2}$     ....(1)

Another expression for $\frac{\omega}{v}$ is obtained from the given information that rod makes one revolution by the time centre reaches the dot.

${\omega}t =2 \pi \text { and } \mathrm{vt}=\mathrm{d} \quad \Rightarrow \frac{\omega}{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{2 \pi}{\mathrm{~d}}$        ....(2)
from equation 1 and 2: $\frac{12 \mathrm{x}}{\ell^2}=\frac{2 \pi}{d}$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{x}=\frac{\pi{\ell^2}}{6 \mathrm{~d}}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q:
Paragraph: A smooth disc of mass M and radius R can rotate freely around a vertical axis supported by frictionless bearings. A constraining vertical surface of negligible mass, whose shape is a semicircular $\operatorname{arc}$ of radius $\mathrm{r}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{2}$, is fixed on the disc as shown in the figure. A small ball of mass $m$ is placed on the stationary disc and is bowled at a speed $v$ in such a way that it reaches the internal side of the constraining surface tangentially.
Question: Mark the CORRECT statement :-
options: Momentum of ball \& disc system is conserved
Mechanical energy of ball \& disc system is not conserved
Angular momentum of ball \& disc system is conserved
Mechanical energy of ball \& disc system is not conserved due to impulsive reaction from support bearings
questions.solution:
Let ball leaves with velocity u \& disc rotates with $\omega$ clockwise direction Angular moment conservation about centre of disc
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{L}}_{\mathrm{i}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{L}}_{\mathrm{f}}$
$\mathrm{O}=\operatorname{muR}-\left(\frac{\mathrm{MR}^2}{2}\right) \omega$
$\omega=\frac{2 \mathrm{mu}}{\mathrm{MR}}$ $\ldots(i)$
Energy conservation
$\frac{1}{2} m v^2=\frac{1}{2} m u^2+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I} \omega^2$ $\ldots(ii)$
$u=v \sqrt{\frac{M}{M+2 m}}, \omega=2 \frac{m v}{M R} \sqrt{\frac{M}{M+2 m}}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q:
Paragraph: A smooth disc of mass M and radius R can rotate freely around a vertical axis supported by frictionless bearings. A constraining vertical surface of negligible mass, whose shape is a semicircular $\operatorname{arc}$ of radius $\mathrm{r}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{2}$, is fixed on the disc as shown in the figure. A small ball of mass $m$ is placed on the stationary disc and is bowled at a speed $v$ in such a way that it reaches the internal side of the constraining surface tangentially.
Question: What is speed of the ball when it leaves the disc :-
questions.solution:
Let ball leaves with velocity u \& disc rotates with $\omega$ clockwise direction Angular moment conservation about centre of disc
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{L}}_{\mathrm{i}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{L}}_{\mathrm{f}}$
$\mathrm{O}=\operatorname{muR}-\left(\frac{\mathrm{MR}^2}{2}\right) \omega$
$\omega=\frac{2 \mathrm{mu}}{\mathrm{MR}}$ $\ldots(i)$
Energy conservation
$\frac{1}{2} m v^2=\frac{1}{2} m u^2+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I} \omega^2$ $\ldots(ii)$
$u=v \sqrt{\frac{M}{M+2 m}}, \omega=2 \frac{m v}{M R} \sqrt{\frac{M}{M+2 m}}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q:
Paragraph: A smooth disc of mass M and radius R can rotate freely around a vertical axis supported by frictionless bearings. A constraining vertical surface of negligible mass, whose shape is a semicircular $\operatorname{arc}$ of radius $\mathrm{r}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{2}$, is fixed on the disc as shown in the figure. A small ball of mass $m$ is placed on the stationary disc and is bowled at a speed $v$ in such a way that it reaches the internal side of the constraining surface tangentially.
Question: What is angular velocity of disc when ball leaves it :-
questions.solution:
Let ball leaves with velocity u \& disc rotates with $\omega$ clockwise direction Angular moment conservation about centre of disc
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{L}}_{\mathrm{i}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{L}}_{\mathrm{f}}$
$\mathrm{O}=\operatorname{muR}-\left(\frac{\mathrm{MR}^2}{2}\right) \omega$
$\omega=\frac{2 \mathrm{mu}}{\mathrm{MR}}$ $\ldots(i)$
Energy conservation
$\frac{1}{2} m v^2=\frac{1}{2} m u^2+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I} \omega^2$ $\ldots(ii)$
$u=v \sqrt{\frac{M}{M+2 m}}, \omega=2 \frac{m v}{M R} \sqrt{\frac{M}{M+2 m}}$

Paragraph: A solid cylinder of mass $m$ and radius $R$ is kept at rest on a plank of mass 2 m lying on a smooth horizontal surface. Massless and inextensible string connecting cylinder to the plank is passing over a massless pulley. The friction between the cylinder and the plank is sufficient to prevent slipping. Pulley A is pulled with a constant horizontal force F .
Question: Magnitude of friction force acting on the plank is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q:
Paragraph: Greek lores have a fable of Sisyphus, a celebrated philosopher. He was punished in Hades for rebelling against the king. The punishment was to roll a spherical stone up a hill from where it would invariably roll down to the other side. He would run after the stone, to catch it back and roll it back uphill. The top of the hill was always a position of unstable equilibrium. The sphere and Sisyphus have same mass. Being a great philosopher, Sisyphus compared this act with our attempts to succeed in life. No matter how high we go, the stone of success always rolls down back. Philosophy apart, answer the following questions.
Question: At what height from the centre should sisyphus push in a direction parallel to hill to roll the sphere slowly with minimum force.
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q:
Paragraph: Greek lores have a fable of Sisyphus, a celebrated philosopher. He was punished in Hades for rebelling against the king. The punishment was to roll a spherical stone up a hill from where it would invariably roll down to the other side. He would run after the stone, to catch it back and roll it back uphill. The top of the hill was always a position of unstable equilibrium. The sphere and Sisyphus have same mass. Being a great philosopher, Sisyphus compared this act with our attempts to succeed in life. No matter how high we go, the stone of success always rolls down back. Philosophy apart, answer the following questions.
Question: If the sphere is rolling down, and Sisyphus wants to run along with the same acceleration without exerting any force on it.
options: friction on Sisyphus would be backwards and equal to that on sphere
friction on Sisyphus would be forwards and greater than on sphere
friction on Sisyphus would be backwards and less than on sphere
friction on Sisyphus would be forwards and equal to that on sphere
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q:
Paragraph: Greek lores have a fable of Sisyphus, a celebrated philosopher. He was punished in Hades for rebelling against the king. The punishment was to roll a spherical stone up a hill from where it would invariably roll down to the other side. He would run after the stone, to catch it back and roll it back uphill. The top of the hill was always a position of unstable equilibrium. The sphere and Sisyphus have same mass. Being a great philosopher, Sisyphus compared this act with our attempts to succeed in life. No matter how high we go, the stone of success always rolls down back. Philosophy apart, answer the following questions.
Question: What is the minimum friction coefficient on the hill for Sisyphus to just push the sphere up (assume it to be same for Sisyphus and Rock.) He pushes in a direction parallel to the hill.
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{F}_{\min } \Rightarrow \mathrm{F} \text { at } \mathrm{R}, \alpha=0, \mathrm{a}=0 \\ & \mathrm{mg} \sin \theta+\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{f}_1 \quad \rightarrow \text { sisyphus } \\ & \mathrm{F}+\mathrm{f}_2=\mathrm{mg} \sin \theta \quad \rightarrow \text { stone } \\ & \mathrm{FR}=\mathrm{f}_2 \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \text { Stone } \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{F}=\mathrm{f}_2 \quad \Rightarrow \mathrm{~F}=\frac{\mathrm{mg} \sin \theta}{2}=\mathrm{f}_2 \leq \mu \mathrm{mg} \cos \theta \\ & \quad \begin{array}{l} \mu \leq \tan \theta / 2 \end{array} \\ & \text { for sisyphus, } \\ & \quad \begin{array}{l} \text { mgsin } \theta \mathrm{F}=\mathrm{f}_1 \leq \mu \mathrm{mg} \cos \theta \\ \quad \frac{3 \mathrm{mg} \sin \theta}{2} \leq \mu \mathrm{mg} \cos \theta \end{array} \quad \Rightarrow \mu \geq 3 / 2 \tan \theta \end{aligned}$ For stone to roll as well as sisyphus to not $\operatorname{slip} \mu \geq 3 / 2 \tan \theta$.
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
